title: The Macro Minute: Did the White House just signal WWIII?

Happy good Friday out there team 42.
It's your skipper here Darius Delta
presenting our macro minute for Friday,
April 3rd, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is
did the White House just signal World
War III?
Our answer is per our fundamental
research summary, which is derived from
thousands of slides of institutional
grade analysis that we unpack with
highly regarded institutional investors
each month.
Our research views the probability of a
US fiscal crisis or total war involving
the US within the next 5 to 7 years as
greater than 50%. We may have to revise
this timeline to 5 to 7 months.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle.
The weak negative impulses in private
sector labor income and nominal wage and
salary compensation do not support our
resilient US economy or paradigm see
themes and support our jobless recovery
theme.
The weak negative impulse in private
sector employment and strong negative
impulse in private sector average hourly
earnings support our jobless recovery
theme. The strong negative impulses in
the civilian labor force, the labor
force participation rate, and the
employment to population ratio support
our jobless recovery theme.
Private sector job openings contracting
nearly 15 times faster than labor supply
on a year-over-year basis also supports
our jobless recovery theme.
The dramatic underperformance of
employment growth relative to the growth
rates of labor supply, corporate
profits, retained earnings, and
equipment investment, all of which are
phenomenal leading indicators for
employment growth content supports our
jobless recovery theme.
There's been no growth in true private
services payrolls, which are 54% of the
total, since the second half of 2023.
Reminder, the Fed is still applying
downward pressure on already slowing
labor market via a restrictive policy
rate.
The Fed's still restrictive policy rate
increases the probability of a jobless
recovery.
Thankfully, the strong positive impulse
impulses in temporary employment and
strong negative impulses in cyclical
unemployment and long-term unemployment
confirm the low probability of a
developing recession in the US economy
signaled by jobless claims, the 42 macro
fat five recession signaling indicators,
and the 42 macro business cycle model.
In China, the March unofficial composite
PMI data did not support our outlook for
firming growth. In Japan, the March
final composite PMI data did not support
our outlook for firming growth. Nothing
actionable with respect to our Bayesian
priors with respect to the inflation
cycle or the monetary policy cycle. So,
shifting to the fiscal policy cycle,
President Trump petitioned Congress for
$2.2 trillion discretionary budget that
materially boost defense spending.
The White House's budget proposal
outlines $1.5 trillion in funding for
defense in fiscal 2027 versus $1
trillion requested for fiscal 2026.
50% [laughter] increase.
The figure comprises $1.1 trillion in
base Department of Defense discretionary
spending plus an additional 350 billion
in mandatory expenditures tied to the
US-Israel-Iran conflict. Additionally,
the budget proposal calls for 10% for a
10% reduction in non-defense
discretionary spending, which would
amount to roughly $73 billion.
Per White House Budget Director Russell
Vought, President Trump promised to
reinvest in America's national security
infrastructure to make our nation safe
in a dangerous world that they're
actively making [clears throat] less
safe.
Going back to his quote, my apologies
for the editorialism there. Though I do
get paid to editorialize, lots of money.
The 2027 budget upholds this promise and
would ensure that the United States
continues to maintain the world's most
powerful and capable military.
More on that later. FYI, expenditures on
national defense are annualizing at 988
billion on a fiscal year-to-date basis
here in fiscal 2026.
POTUS's demand of $1.5 trillion in
fiscal 2027, which begins in October,
not in 2 to 3 weeks,
represents a historic percentage
increase, the likes of which have not
been seen since World War II.
Let me say that again.
POTUS's demand of $1.5 trillion in
fiscal 2027, which is a 50-plus percent
increase from what he requested in
fiscal 2026,
represents a historic percentage
increase, the likes of which have not
been seen since World War II.
Reminder, fiscal largesse is
perpetuating the K-shaped economy crisis
through two channels. Number one, a
historic Cantillon effect, and number
two, an equally historic crowding out of
the private sector by excessive public
sector borrowing.
Regarding the former, the Cantillon
effect, the federal government spends
roughly 80% more on everything not
including Social Security and Medicare,
like net interest and national defense,
than it spends on poor families via
means-tested programs.
So, if you're poor, someone like me or
the folks that I left behind when I got
made it out of the hood, and made it out
of the homeless shelter, and made it out
of the sleeping in vans,
we get roughly about $1.4 trillion a
year from from the government.
The government spends another $2.6
trillion a year on things like net
national defense, net interest, and all
the other stuff that doesn't go to poor
people
outside of what it spends on Social
Security and Medicare. So, there's a
narrative in this country that the poor
and the lazy are the problem with the
debt, and that narrative is just
flat out being rejected by the data. I
mean, just I know a lot of people
millions of people get their, you know,
political views pumped into their heads
in the likes of Fox News or CNBC or
Newsmax or or CNN.

Transition to our 42 macro dashboard. As
always, we wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled vol
regime classification. It says, "Why
does it seem harder for equities?"
It says, "Hey Darius, hope all is well.
Loved last week's around the horn and
looking forward always to the upcoming
one.
I've been spending a lot of time
thinking about vol regime classification
across different asset classes and I
keep running into something that
genuinely puzzles me. For currencies, it
seems relatively straightforward. You
can take something like CVX and compare
a short-term moving average against a
longer-term one, in which you get a
clean read on whether you're in a high
or low vol environment. But when I try
to apply the same kind of thinking to
equity spy specifically, it breaks down
dramatically. VIX doesn't seem to behave
the same way as a regime classifier. The
transitions are messier and the signal
is noisier. I've tried various lookback
windows and smoothing methods, but the
equity vol regime problem seems
fundamentally different. Is there
something about the nature of equity
volatility that makes the regime
classification harder? Is the mean
reverting behavior of the VIX or the
clustering of vol spikes? Is it the mean
reverting behavior of the VIX or the
clustering of vol spikes? Or is it just
the right approach for equities? Equity
is structurally different from what
works for other asset classes,
currencies, bonds, etc. Would love to
hear your perspective. Thanks always up
the incredible work. I'd be in a serious
tizzy right now if it wasn't for KISS
and Dr. Mo. So, thank you to the member
for the incredibly thoughtful question.
These are the types of institutional
grade questions that we are unpacking
together and learning together here in
our global invest community featuring
thousands of investors all across the
sophistication spectrum in 80 plus
countries around the world. Some of the
top buy side portfolio managers and and
analysts on global Wall Street are in
here participating with some of the most
novice retail investors in the world.
You know what? That's phenomenal because
we exist, 42 macro exist to completely
eradicate the K-shaped nature of global
financial markets.
And if we don't ever we don't succeed on
that, well, we're certainly succeed on

we currently serve. But if we if we
don't succeed on that, if I die before

totally fine cuz it's it's a noble goal
to die for.
And so getting back and answering this
question, so the first thing I'll say is
we don't use the VIX to to determine the
volatility regime classification for for
stocks. So that's the first thing I'll
say on this. Secondarily,
the the subscriber is hinting at the
behavior of equities cuz all the the
behavior of equities and how volatility
clusters in the asset class and how all
volatility time series mean revert for
all the asset classes, but they're
hinting at something that's also
important to note to the extent you're
building models like this, which is
all the asset classes behave
differently.
You have to study each asset class and
each factor within each asset class
independently
to derive a constructive and predictive
signal from the perspective of our
volatility just momentum signal, which
we use to

use those volatility just momentum
signals as as independent models as
features within our global macro matrix

itself is part of the

KISS and Dr. Mo. So, if you you know,
you got to build a really you know,
credible, defensible, thoughtful, and
ultimately predictive
volatility momentum model if you want to
manage risk like a like you know, like
an institution would on the global buy
side, like one of the top institutions
would across the global buy side. So,
let me just show you share with you a
couple statistics on you know, what
we're doing here from a volatility just
momentum signal perspective and how that
feeds into our other models and and
helps us create fantastic results
through the lens of KISS, which is
filtering our market regime signals to
retail investors with a very simple,
easy to follow asset allocation
featuring 60% stocks, 30% gold, 10%
Bitcoin when it's maxed out, and Dr. Mo,
which is filtering those same market
regime signals to institutional
investors across 70 different factors in
12 different asset classes. So,
if you look at our volatility just
momentum signal for stocks, this is the
S&P 500 backtest right here.
What this backtest highlights is the
percentage of cumulative performance by
the VAMs condition dating back to the
start of 1998. And so when the S&P 500
when our volatility just momentum signal
is is suggesting that the S&P 500 is
bullish, is signaling that S&P 500 is
bullish, that's where 95% of the S&P
500's return has come from since January
1998. We use daily log price changes to
sum sum daily log price changes so that
we don't anchor the backtest on one time
series. You know, that's that's again
this institutional grade risk management
and backtesting. And so that so the key
takeaway is that you know, if all you
did with the S&P 500 was just be long
when our when our when our volatility
just momentum signal is saying be long
the S&P 500, you would have captured 95%
of the upside in the asset class.
That same statistic is 122%
from Bitcoin as far back as we have the
data, which I believe begins in
Bloomberg some I think in July of 2010.
And so as I mentioned, we use our
volatility just momentum signal to
filter into our market regime nowcasting
process, which is the top-down risk
management overlay featured in KISS and
Dr. Mo, and that's creates incredibly
positive results as well. So, if you
look at the percentage of return,
percentage of cumulative performance by
market regime, 67%
of the S&P 500's return has come when
our market regime nowcasting process has
indicated we're in a risk-on Goldilocks
market regime. 31% has come when our
market regime nowcasting process has
enter has as as indicated we're in a
risk-off risk-on rather in reflation
market regime. Only 6% has come when
we're in a risk-off inflation market
regime and then only four and minus 4%
has come when we're in a risk-off
deflation market regime. Those figures
are 56% for Goldilocks and 62% for
reflation for Bitcoin. So, that means
you know,
a cumulative 118% of Bitcoin's return
has come from has come when we're in our
market regime nowcasting process has
indicated we're in a risk-on regime.
That the figure is 98% for the S&P 500.
Minus 18% of Bitcoin's cumulative
performance, so that means Bitcoin goes
down when our market regime nowcasting
process indicates that we're in a
risk-off market regime.
You know, so and that number is is very
negligible, you know,
I think 2% when we're in a risk-off
market regime for the S&P 500. Another
way to you can tell that this process is
incredibly effective is the decay in
Sharpe ratios when you go from a risk-on
regime, 0.9 0.6 to 0.1 to zero from a
risk-on regime to a risk-off regime in
the S&P 500 and from 0.9 and 1.2 in
Goldilocks and reflation to minus 0.7
and minus 0.1
in inflation and deflation, which are
risk-off market regimes, risk-off the
inflation bias being inflation, risk-off
with the disinflationary bias being
deflation. And so you know, the reason
this stuff you know, we can determine
that this stuff works by looking at the
rolling out of sample backtest for for
stocks for instance. When Dr. Mo is
accumulating signaling to our customers
that hey, you should be long the S&P and
Dr. Mo what Dr. Mo does is very similar
to KISS in terms of incorporating the
top-down risk management overlay for
volatility targeting and the bottom-up
risk management overlay for dynamic
position sizing. When Dr. Mo says you
need to be long the S&P 500
125% of the S&P 500's cumulative return
since January 2025 January 1998 has come
when Dr. Mo is saying be long the S&P
500. And so what that ultimately means
is that if the S&P 500 went up, let's
just say for round number's sake, $100
since January 1998, you would have
actually made $125
just being long when you're long it and
not being long when you're not supposed
to be long it. That statistic is
125% as well for Bitcoin. And so the
reason you can generate more return that
even exist in the asset class is because
you are creating a positively skewed
return distribution and chopping off the
left side of the left tail of the
distribution by avoiding volatility drag
and destabilizing drawdowns and in what
we would otherwise call type two errors
in institutional risk management speak.
And so by avoiding type two errors and
creating a positively skewed return
distribution in your in your portfolio
or in each of these factors or in any of
these asset classes, you're able to
compound returns from a higher net asset
value. And I don't know if you guys know
this little dirty little secret about
compounding and exponential growth, but
when you have a higher quantity, the
same percentage change increases the
quantity faster.
And so this is a dirty little secret of
of of of institutional risk management,
which is if you avoid volatility drag,
your wealth compounds faster. Your
clients' wealth compounds faster. And
ultimately your bonuses compound faster.
